text: Realisation d'un bypass robotique associé la cure d'une hernie hiatale et renforcement par bioprothèse. Il s'agit d'une patiente de cinquante-cinquante, qui présente une obésité, stade trois avec IMCA quarante, associée une hernie hiatale et esophagite.

The surgical team uses three robotic trocars and two assistant trocars, positioning the robot in cytokine. They target a type III hiatal hernia with a biased hiatal orifice, beginning dissection of the hernia sacs using monopolar hooks. The lipoma contained within the sacs is visible. The team approaches the flaccid part of the small epiblot to release the right and left pillars of the diaphragm, creating a retroesophageal window. The esophagus is repositioned in an intra-abdominal position, and the hiatal hole is sutured with non-resolvable and braided threads, reducing its size with a pre-esophageal point.

The surgeon reinforces the hiatal orifice with a pre-cut bioprothesis based on collagen, securing it with monofilament threads at a slow speed. The bioprothesis is fixed onto the pillars and diaphragm, visibly reinforcing the hiatal hole.

The surgeon performs a gastric bypass using robotic instruments, creating a proximal gastric pouch with an endogea and violet graft. The gestational anus is checked and sectioned, followed by the restoration of digestive continuity through gastro-gestational anastomosis. Endocyanin worms and fluorescence are used to assess vascularization and tissue quality. Openings are made in the proximal stomach and genome, with sutures performed using cracked wires at slow speed. The gastro-regional anastomosis is calibrated with a nasogastric probe, measuring fifteen millimeters in diameter. A food lance of one hundred and fifty centimeters is created, and an anastomosis is made at the lance foot using a GIA endo with brown graphite. The mechanical clamp holes are closed, along with the mesenteric gaps and Petersen's space using non-resolvable braided thread.

This is the final view of a gastric bypass done with a hiatal hernia cure and reinforcement by a bioprothesis. The post-operative follow-up was quite simple. We see the TOGD at J-I, the patient came out at J-III. Six months ago, the patient is asymptomatic.
